title: Psychology as a way of thinking
objective: Learn the core mental models psychologists actually reason with - operational definitions, the dual-process model, base rates, and reconstructive memory - and how each one changes the way you explain human behaviour.
beginnerExplanation: Psychology is the scientific study of mind and behaviour, but the harder part is learning to think like a psychologist rather than just collecting facts about people. The central move is this: do not trust the obvious explanation for why someone acted, because people are unreliable narrators of their own minds. A famous demonstration is the dual-process model popularised by Daniel Kahneman and Amos Tversky: a fast, automatic mode (call it System 1) that jumps to conclusions, and a slow, effortful mode (System 2) that checks them. Try this yourself. A bat and a ball cost $1.10 together, and the bat costs $1.00 more than the ball. Most people instantly answer that the ball costs 10 cents - that is System 1. The correct answer is 5 cents (the bat is then $1.05, totalling $1.10). The first answer feels true and is wrong, which is exactly the gap psychology studies. Thinking like a psychologist means treating your own intuition as data to be tested, not as the truth.
deeperExplanation: A mature psychological thinker reasons with a small set of mental models, not a pile of named effects. First, operational definitions: before you can study 'happiness' or 'aggression' you must define it as something measurable - a score on a scale, a reaction time, a count of behaviours - and accept that the measure is never the whole thing. Second, base rates and the difference between within-person and between-person claims: a finding that holds on average across thousands of people may say nothing about any single person in front of you. Third, reconstructive memory: Elizabeth Loftus showed that memory is not a recording but a rebuild that happens at the moment of recall, so a vivid, confident memory can be entirely false - in her studies, simply asking how fast cars were going when they 'smashed' versus 'hit' changed what people later remembered seeing. Fourth, and most important for honesty, the field is going through a genuine reckoning. In the 2010s the replication crisis revealed that many celebrated findings - ego depletion, several social-priming results - did not reproduce when other labs ran them carefully. The leading response has been pre-registration, larger samples, and open data, but how much of older psychology survives is still being worked out. So thinking like a psychologist now means holding two things at once: the models are powerful, and you must ask 'has this replicated?' before you build on it.
keyTakeaways: The core habit is distrusting the obvious explanation - people are poor narrators of their own minds, so intuition becomes data to test, not truth to report.
The dual-process model (fast, automatic System 1 vs slow, effortful System 2) explains why an answer can feel certain and still be wrong, as the bat-and-ball puzzle shows.
Nothing is studied until it is operationally defined - turned into something you can measure - and the measure is never the whole concept.
Average, between-person findings rarely predict a specific individual; mixing the two up is one of the most common reasoning errors.
Memory is reconstructive, not a recording (Loftus); confidence and vividness are not evidence that a memory is accurate.
Because of the replication crisis, 'has this replicated?' is now part of thinking like a psychologist, not an optional footnote.
example: Take a real workplace claim: 'Our top salesperson is great with people, so we should promote them to manage the team.' A psychological thinker breaks this open. First, operational definition: what does 'great with people' mean - closing deals, or developing other people? Those are different skills. Second, base rate vs individual: managing is a different role, and being above average at selling tells you little about coaching ability (the correlation between the two is weak). Third, the fundamental attribution error - we explain the salesperson's success by their character ('they're just a natural') and ignore the situation (an easy territory, warm leads, a good product). The famous Peter Principle, that people rise to their level of incompetence, is partly this reasoning failure made into a promotion policy. The psychological read is: do not promote on a trait you have not actually measured for the new job.
commonMistakes: Treating intuition as evidence - assuming that because an explanation for behaviour feels obvious, it must be correct (the bat-and-ball trap).
Confusing a between-person average with a within-person prediction, for example using a group finding to make a confident claim about one specific person.
Trusting confident, vivid memories as accurate records, when Loftus showed memory is rebuilt at recall and easily distorted by how a question is phrased.
Citing a famous study (Stanford Prison Experiment, ego depletion, social priming) as settled fact without asking whether it replicated or was methodologically sound.
exercise: Take one belief you hold about why a person in your life behaves the way they do (a colleague, a family member, yourself). Write the explanation down. Then attack it with three of the models from this lesson: (1) Operational definition - what would you actually have to measure to test this? (2) Situation vs character - what features of their situation could explain the behaviour without any reference to their personality? (3) Reconstructive memory - is your belief built on a few vivid remembered episodes that you might be misremembering? Write a revised, more cautious explanation and note what evidence would change your mind.

mentalModel.title: Intuition is the hypothesis, not the verdict
mentalModel.summary: Psychologists treat the first, obvious explanation for behaviour as a guess to be tested rather than the answer. The work is converting a vague intuition into something measurable, checking it against situation as well as character, and asking whether the effect survives replication.
anchors: System 1 vs System 2 (the fast wrong answer vs the slow checked one)
operational definitions (turn the concept into a measurement)
situation over character (the fundamental attribution error)

howItWorks.title: How a psychologist reasons through a behaviour claim
steps: State the everyday explanation in plain words ('they did X because they are Y').
Operationalise it: what exactly would you measure to test 'Y', and on what scale?
Separate situation from character - list situational forces that could produce the same behaviour with no trait involved.
Check the level of claim: is this an average across many people, or a prediction about this one person? Do not swap them.
Ask the replication question: is the effect you are leaning on robust, or one of the findings that failed to reproduce in the 2010s?

upsides: Gives you a reliable defence against being fooled by confident-but-wrong intuitions, your own included.
Forces vague claims about people into testable form, which is what separates psychology from folk wisdom and horoscopes.
downsides: Operationalising a rich human concept always loses something - a happiness score is not happiness, and forgetting that produces brittle conclusions.
The models can be applied too mechanically; real behaviour is shaped by culture, context, and history that a clean experiment strips away.
teaching.workedExampleLabel: Worked example: reading Milgram without the myth
teaching.workedExample: Stanley Milgram's 1961 obedience experiments are usually summarised as 'about 65% of participants delivered the maximum 450-volt shock to a stranger when an authority told them to.' The lazy reading is 'people are secretly monsters' - a character explanation. The psychological reading uses situation over character. Milgram varied the setup across conditions: when the experimenter gave orders by phone instead of in the room, obedience dropped sharply; when another 'participant' refused, most followed the rebel and disobeyed. The same people behaved completely differently as the situation changed, which is the actual finding - obedience is a property of the situation, not a fixed personal flaw. Even the famous 65% figure deserves the replication question: it comes from one specific condition, the studies could not be run that way today on ethical grounds, and historians have since shown Milgram's lab nudged participants more than the tidy story admits. So the honest takeaway is layered: the situational power is real and has been partially replicated, but the single dramatic number is one condition out of many, not a law of human nature.
realWorldApplications: Hiring and promotion decisions, where separating 'great at the current job' from 'will be great at the new job' avoids the Peter Principle.
Eyewitness testimony in courts, where Loftus's work on reconstructive memory has reshaped how confident identifications are weighed.
Product and interface design, where dual-process thinking explains why users act on fast intuition rather than reading instructions.
Public health and behaviour change, where base-rate reasoning stops a campaign from over-claiming on the back of a small, unreplicated study.
whoWorksWithThis: Research and academic psychologists who design experiments and now pre-register them to survive the replication crisis.
Clinical psychologists who must separate a client's reconstructed account from what the evidence supports.
Organisational psychologists applying these models to hiring, team behaviour, and workplace motivation.

quiz.question: A psychologist hears 'our best salesperson should be promoted to manager because they are clearly great with people.' Which reasoning move is most central to evaluating this claim well?
options: Trust the manager's intuition, since they know the team best
Separate the trait being measured ('great at selling') from the trait the new job needs ('great at developing people'), because the two correlate weakly
Assume strong performance reflects fixed character and will transfer to any role
Promote first and measure the outcome later
quiz.explanation: The everyday claim swaps one skill for another and reads success as fixed character. The psychological move is to operationally define the trait the new role actually needs and notice that selling ability is a weak predictor of coaching ability - the reasoning failure behind the Peter Principle.

quiz.question: Why do psychologists now routinely ask 'has this replicated?' before relying on a famous finding?
options: Because all old psychology has been proven false
Because replication is a legal requirement for publishing
Because the 2010s replication crisis showed several celebrated results, such as ego depletion and some social-priming studies, failed to reproduce in careful re-runs
Because newer studies are always more correct than older ones
quiz.explanation: The replication crisis revealed that a number of well-known effects did not hold up when other labs repeated them carefully. This did not falsify the whole field, but it made 'does this replicate?' a standard part of psychological reasoning, alongside larger samples and pre-registration.

perspectives.researchPsychologist: My instinct on any striking result is suspicion before excitement. Since the replication crisis I pre-register my hypotheses and analysis plan before I see the data, because I know how easily I can talk myself into a pattern that is really just noise. A finding I cannot operationally define and another lab cannot reproduce is a story, not a result.
perspectives.clinicalPsychologist: A client's account of their own history is the most important thing in the room and also the least reliable, because memory rebuilds itself every time it is recalled. My job is not to correct them but to hold their narrative gently and notice where situation, not character, is doing the real work - so we change what can actually be changed.
perspectives.organisationalPsychologist: Businesses constantly promote on traits they never measured and explain behaviour by personality when the incentive structure is the real cause. I spend most of my time pulling people back from character explanations to situational ones, because you can redesign a workflow far more easily than you can redesign a person.
